title: Managing livestock during a movement standstill
subtitle: What you can and can't do while the clock is running after new stock arrives.
excerpt: A movement standstill starts the moment new cattle, sheep, goats, or pigs arrive on your holding. For most species it lasts six days; for pigs it's twenty. During that period, many movements off the holding are restricted. Knowing what is and isn't allowed keeps you legal and prevents accidental standstill violations.
sourceNotes: DEFRA/APHA The Livestock (Record, Identification and Movement) Order 2015. APHA movement rules guidance (gov.uk). BCMS cattle movement rules.

glossaryTerms.definition: A legally required period after new stock arrives on a holding during which most movements off the holding are restricted; intended to limit disease spread.

content.text: is a disease-traceability measure: if a disease is identified on a farm that sold you animals, APHA can trace back through recent movements and halt further spread. For sheep, goats, and cattle, the standstill is six days from the arrival date. For pigs, it's 20 days. The standstill applies to the entire holding, not just to the species that arrived.
Moving sheep, cattle, goats, or pigs off the holding to another holding (including to market)
For pigs: moving any pig off the holding for any reason during the 20-day standstill
Direct to slaughter at a licensed abattoir, this is always permitted (with a movement licence)
Emergency vet trips: a sick animal can be moved for urgent treatment
Poultry and rabbits are not covered by the standstill rules

Day zero is the day the animals arrive. Day one is the following day. The standstill ends at the start of day seven (for a six-day standstill). If animals arrive on a Monday, the standstill ends on the following Sunday and movements can proceed from Monday morning. If you're planning to send animals to a sale that falls on day five, reschedule the purchase.

Record the arrival in your holding register immediately: species, number, ear-tag numbers or batch ID, date and time of arrival, the holding the animals came from (their CPH number). This record is the legal start of the standstill clock and is what APHA checks during a visit.
